criteria_results.title: Allegation 1: Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies cherry-picking/favorable trade allocation as a distinct allegation category. It explicitly describes the pattern of disproportionately allocating profitable trades to Oceanic Fund I LP (68.4% profitable allocation rate) and Chao's personal account (81.6% profitable allocation rate), compared to 41.2% for other client accounts. The output includes dedicated sections titled 'Cherry-picking trade allocations,' 'Cherry-picking favoring Chao's personal account,' 'Cherry-picking favoring Oceanic Fund I LP,' and 'Cherry-picking allocated to Oceanic Fund I LP,' with detailed statistical analysis, dollar figures ($7.8M and $438K in excess profits), and statutory citations (Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act).

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Correct Statutory Provisions Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly and repeatedly cites Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 in connection with the cherry-picking allegation. For example, it states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' The output also maps specific respondents (Whitmore, Chao, WCA) to these provisions in the Discrepancies and Defense Issues section, and discusses the scienter requirements under Section 206(1) versus the negligence standard under Section 206(2) in the context of the cherry-picking charges.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: WCA Identified as Implicated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies WCA (Whitmore Capital Advisors LLC) as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation. In the 'Cherry-picking trade allocations' section, the output states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' Additionally, in the 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure' section, it notes 'WCA investment adviser; engaged in cherry-picking through actions and omissions of Whitmore and Chao.' The 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section also maps WCA to the cherry-picking conduct. WCA is clearly and repeatedly identified as an implicated party in the cherry-picking allegation.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Whitmore Identified as Implicated (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to his 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP. Multiple sections confirm this: the 'Favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest' section states 'Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million'; the 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure' section lists Whitmore as implicated in 'Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations, as the individual who benefited through his 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP)'; and the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section maps the cherry-picking conduct to Whitmore under Section 206(1) scienter requirements.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Chao Identified as Implicated (Personal Account)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies Diana L. Chao as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to her personal account receiving favorable allocations. Multiple sections explicitly state this: 'Cherry-picking allocated to Chao's personal account' section details that her personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) received 87 allocations from omnibus fills with a 81.6% profitable allocation rate versus 41.2% for other accounts, generating $438,000 in excess profits. The 'Individuals at Risk' section states Chao 'personally benefited from cherry-picking through favorable allocations to her personal account.' The allegation category mapping confirms 'Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations, as the Head of Trading who was directly responsible for allocation decisions and who personally benefited from the cherry-picking pattern through favorable allocations to her personal account).'

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the false statement in Item 6 of WCA's Form ADV Part 2A. criteria_results.title: Form ADV: Omission in Item 11 Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify omissions in Item 11 of the Form ADV Part 2A. The agent's output discusses Item 12 (brokerage practices), Item 6 (performance-based fees), and general Form ADV disclosure failures, but does not specifically identify Item 11 omissions regarding Whitmore's LP interest or Chao's personal account receiving omnibus allocations. The agent mentions Item 11 only in passing references to the Code of Ethics generally, without identifying the specific omissions required by the criterion.

criteria_results.title: Allegation 3: Deficient Compliance Policies and Procedures â€” Identified

criteria_results.title: Deficient Compliance Policies: Rule 206(4)-7 Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references Rule 206(4)-7 under the Investment Advisers Act multiple times in connection with the deficient compliance policies allegation. ALLEGATION CATEGORY 3 --- DEFICIENT WRITTEN POLICIES AND PROCEDURES' and repeatedly cites Rule 206(4)-7 throughout the document in connection with this allegation.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 7.3 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 7.3 deficiency in the 'Deviation from allocation procedures' section, stating: 'Section 7.3 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Trade Allocation," described a "rotation" methodology for the allocation of IPO (initial public offering) and secondary offering allocations among eligible client accounts. However, Section 7.3 contained no procedures whatsoever for the allocation of equity trades executed through omnibus accounts.' This directly satisfies the criterion by identifying the deficiency by section number and describing that it covered only IPO/secondary allocation rotation while lacking procedures for omnibus equity trade allocation.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 9.1 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 9.1 deficiency by section number and substance. In the 'Personal Trading Compliance Requirement' section, the output states: 'Section 9.1 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Personal Trading," required employees to pre-clear trades in individual securities and to report personal securities transactions on a quarterly basis... However, Section 9.1 did not address or restrict employees from receiving allocations from the Firm's omnibus account in their personal brokerage accounts. This gap in the personal trading policy allowed Chao to receive 87 allocations from omnibus fills in her personal brokerage account... without triggering any compliance review, pre-clearance requirement, or approval process.' This directly satisfies the criterion by identifying Section 9.1's pre-clearance requirement and its failure to address omnibus allocations.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Omnibus Trading Volume Percentage Cited

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies failure to maintain required books and records as a distinct allegation category (Allegation Category 4). criteria_results.title: Books and Records: Key Quantitative Facts
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output includes data point (1) — 2,614 of 5,776 omnibus trades (45.2%) lacked contemporaneous allocation records — which appears multiple times. However, the output does not include data point (2) regarding 312 Excel spreadsheets with metadata showing creation dates after corresponding trade dates (backdating), nor data point (3) regarding an average creation lag of 17 business days. Since only 1 of the 3 required data points is clearly present, the criterion requiring at least 2 of 3 is not met.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Trade Count Discrepancy (4,217 vs. 4,312)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the discrepancy between the 4,217 trades analyzed per the referral notice and the 4,312 trades in the OQR dataset, noting this is a difference of 95 trades. This appears in the 'Failure to conduct best execution review of Lakeshore Trading transactions' section: 'The OQR analysis covers the full three-year Review Period (January 2021--December 2023) and identifies a trade universe of 4,312 omnibus equity trades, but the referral notice states only 4,217 were analyzed --- a discrepancy of 95 trades that defense counsel must investigate as a potential methodology gap or data integrity issue that could undermine the statistical conclusions.' The criterion is fully satisfied.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Source Documents Correctly Attributed
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output correctly attributes the 4,217 figure to sec-enforcement-referral-notice.docx and the 4,312 figure to sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx. This is explicitly shown in the 'Favoritism in allocation' section ('The absence of contemporaneous allocation records prevented the: 4,217 (sec-enforcement-referral-notice.docx)') and in multiple 'Key figures' sections under 'Trade Error Log Requirement' and 'Recurring Trade Error Escalation Requirement' ('Observation on Trade Count The omnibus trade dataset contains: 4,312 (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx)'). The discrepancy between these two figures is also flagged as a defense issue in the 'Failure to conduct best execution review' section.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_002: Criminal Obstruction Exposure Flagged (18 U.S.C. Â§Â§ 1505/1519)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly flags parallel criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1519 (up to 20 years) and 18 U.S.C. § 1505 (up to 5 years) in the Discrepancies and Defense Issues section, and specifically notes that 'SEC Enforcement routinely refers obstruction and spoliation conduct to DOJ under parallel-proceedings practice' and that 'counsel should assume a criminal referral is possible.' This directly satisfies the criterion requiring mention of DOJ criminal referral potential under §§ 1505/1519.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Bellini Compensation Calculation Gap ($92,600 vs. $103,800)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output identifies a discrepancy in Bellini's undisclosed compensation figures ($45,000, $47,600, and $92,600 appearing in the referral notice), but it does not identify the specific discrepancy described in the criterion: that the bank records exhibit (ostrowski-bank-records-exhibit.xlsx) shows payments spanning May 2022 through January 2024 (21 months), which at $2,800/month would yield $58,800 in dues plus $45,000 initiation = $103,800 total, versus the referral notice's 17-month calculation of $47,600 + $45,000 = $92,600, creating an ~$11,200 discrepancy between the bank records and the referral notice. The agent treats the three figures ($45,000, $47,600, $92,600) as internally inconsistent within the referral notice itself, but never cross-references the bank records exhibit to identify the 21-month span and the resulting $103,800 figure that conflicts with the referral's $92,600 calculation.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Defense Significance of Compensation Gap Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output identifies a discrepancy in the Bellini compensation figures ($45,000, $47,600, and $92,600 are flagged as three different figures), but the criterion specifically asks about the $92,600 vs. $103,800 discrepancy. The agent never mentions $103,800 at all. The $103,800 figure would represent 17 months × $2,800 = $47,600 in dues plus $45,000 initiation fee... wait, that actually equals $92,600, not $103,800. Regardless, the agent does not discuss the $92,600 vs. $103,800 discrepancy because $103,800 never appears in the output. The agent does note that the three figures ($45,000, $47,600, $92,600) conflict and states this 'directly affect the quantification of undisclosed compensation, which bears on exposure, restitution, and the credibility of the referral's financial analysis,' but this addresses a different discrepancy than the one specified in the criterion ($92,600 vs. $103,800). Since the criterion specifically requires discussion of the $92,600 vs. $103,800 discrepancy and its implications, and the agent never identifies or discusses $103,800, the criterion is not satisfied.

criteria_results.title: Scienter Distinction Between Section 206(1) and 206(2) Flagged
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the scienter distinction between Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) in multiple places. In the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section, it states: 'The referral charges BOTH Section 206(1) --- which requires scienter (intent) --- AND Section 206(2) --- which requires only negligence. This distinction is material to the defense: the 206(1) count can be contested on scienter grounds even where the negligence-based 206(2) count may stand on the same facts.' It further maps specific conduct to the provisions and flags where the charging document leaves the mapping unspecified, noting 'that ambiguity is itself a defense point: demand that the Division commit to which conduct supports the scienter-based count.' The output also explicitly states 'Section 206(1) of the Advisers Act requires proof of SCIENTER (intent to defraud or reckless disregard for the truth). Unlike Section 206(2), a mere negligence showing is insufficient, so the scienter element is a primary defense point on the 206(1) charge.' This fully satisfies the criterion.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_005: Form ADV Disclosure Obligations for Whitmore's LP Interest Analyzed Beyond Restating Allegation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses Whitmore's 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP and the associated conflicts of interest extensively, but it does so by restating the SEC's allegations and analyzing the motive/scienter implications. The output references Item 6 of WCA's Brochure in the context of the cherry-picking allegation (noting it addressed performance-based fees and side-by-side management), but it does not identify Item 6 as a specific Form ADV disclosure obligation applicable to Whitmore's LP interest, nor does it identify Item 11.A requiring disclosure of proprietary interests or conflicts, nor does it reference any SEC Staff guidance or Risk Alerts identifying undisclosed principal investments in pooled vehicles as a high-priority enforcement area. The output merely restates the allegation that WCA's Form ADV failed to disclose the conflict and analyzes the financial incentive structure, without identifying the specific disclosure requirements that would have been triggered by Whitmore's LP interest.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Statute of Limitations Concern Under 28 U.S.C. Â§ 2462
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does raise the five-year statute of limitations under 28 U.S.C. § 2462 in the Discrepancies and Defense Issues section, but it does not apply it to early Review Period conduct (January 2021 onward). Instead, the agent applies the limitations analysis to conduct dated to 2014, 2009, 2008, 2001, and December 31, 2023 — none of which correspond to the January 2021 start of the Review Period. The criterion specifically requires identification that conduct from early 2021 may be approaching or past the five-year limitations period given the October 18, 2024 referral date (i.e., conduct from before October 18, 2019 would be time-barred, and early 2021 conduct would be within the window but approaching it). The agent never makes this specific connection between the Review Period start date (January 1, 2021) and the referral date (October 18, 2024) to flag that early 2021 conduct is approaching the five-year boundary. The agent's statute of limitations analysis is applied to entirely different, much older dates that appear to be hallucinated or misattributed, not to the actual Review Period conduct.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Specific 2021 Conduct Identified as Potentially Time-Barred
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output raises statute of limitations issues under 28 U.S.C. § 2462 in the Discrepancies and Defense Issues section, but the analysis is misapplied. It incorrectly dates conduct to 2014, 2009, 2001, 2008, etc. (which appear to be biographical dates like graduation years or firm formation dates, not the dates of the alleged cherry-picking conduct). The output never specifically identifies Q1 2021 cherry-picking conduct or early 2021 conduct generally as the allegations most at risk for statute of limitations defenses. The Review Period runs January 2021 through December 2023, and the examination commenced March 11, 2024, with the referral on October 18, 2024. A proper analysis would identify that cherry-picking conduct from early 2021 (more than five years before any action is commenced) could be time-barred for civil penalty purposes. Instead, the agent applies the limitations analysis to irrelevant dates and never connects the five-year window to the specific 2021 cherry-picking allegations. The statute of limitations issue is raised only abstractly and incorrectly, without identifying which specific allegations (early 2021 cherry-picking) are at risk.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Date Discrepancy in Obstruction Allegation (April 15 vs. April 22)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses the obstruction allegation and references the April 22, 2024 date for Whitmore's instruction to delete files, and also references the Whitmore-Delgado email as corroborating evidence. The output does note the email's date as 'Mon, 15 Apr 2024 09:23:47 -0400' in passing, but never flags this as a discrepancy with the April 22 date alleged in the referral notice, nor analyzes the significance of this one-week gap as a defense issue.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Defense Significance of Date Discrepancy Discussed
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses the Whitmore deletion instruction and the April 22, 2024 date extensively, including steelmanning innocent readings of the 'clean up the shared drive' instruction. However, the output never mentions or discusses a date discrepancy between April 15 and April 22. The criterion requires discussion of at least one concrete defense implication of the April 15 vs. April 22 date discrepancy specifically — such as the email's earlier date suggesting it predated a specific document request, or that the language 'clean up of legacy files' could support a routine IT maintenance interpretation. The agent's output simply references April 22 as the date of the instruction without ever noting any discrepancy with an April 15 date or drawing any defense implications from such a discrepancy.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Chao's Undisclosed Brokerage Account as Separate Violation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses Chao's personal brokerage account (ending -7823) at Meridian Clearing Corp. extensively in the context of the cherry-picking allegation, but never identifies the specific issue that this account does not appear on WCA's employee personal securities transaction report log (wca-employee-trading-log.xlsx), which lists only a Hartleigh IRA (ending -4401) for Chao. criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Rule 204A-1 (Code of Ethics) Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references Rule 204A-1 multiple times in connection with the failure to disclose employee brokerage accounts. Specifically, it discusses Section 9.1 of the Compliance Manual requiring employees to pre-clear trades and report personal securities transactions 'consistent with the requirements of Rule 204A-1 under the Advisers Act,' and notes that the gap in the personal trading policy 'allowed Chao to receive 87 allocations from omnibus fills in her personal brokerage account...without triggering any compliance review, pre-clearance requirement, or approval process.' The output also references Rule 204A-1's definition of Access Persons and discusses Exhibit H (the employee personal securities transaction report log) as relevant to 'assessing whether Chao's personal trading activity was properly reported under the firm's Code of Ethics and Rule 204A-1.' This connects the undisclosed account issue to a specific regulatory requirement (Rule 204A-1) separate from the cherry-picking allegations.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Omnibus Trade Percentage Discrepancy (73% vs. 78%)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the 73% vs. 78% discrepancy in the 'Cherry-picking trade allocations' section, stating: 'Additionally, the referral notice elsewhere cites a 73% figure, creating an internal inconsistency that defense counsel should examine.' The output also notes that the 78% figure comes from the statistical exhibit (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx) and the 73% figure comes from the referral notice (sec-enforcement-referral-notice.docx), with the 73% figure appearing in the 'Deviation from allocation procedures' section referencing '5,776 omnibus trades out of 7,912 total equity trades.' This satisfies the criterion.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Explanation of Why Omnibus Percentage Discrepancy Matters
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output mentions the 73% vs. 78% discrepancy in passing (noting '78% figure for omnibus trades' and separately referencing '73% figure, creating an internal inconsistency that defense counsel should examine'), but fails to provide any concrete explanation of why the discrepancy matters. The output says only that 'the referral notice elsewhere cites a 73% figure, creating an internal inconsistency that defense counsel should examine' without explaining the implications — e.g., that different denominators or datasets were used, that it suggests unreliable underlying data, or that it could be used to challenge the statistical methodology. The criterion requires at least one concrete explanation of the discrepancy's implications, which is absent.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_010: WCA's Respondeat Superior / Failure to Supervise Liability
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not address WCA's respondeat superior or control person liability specifically for Bellini's directed brokerage conduct, nor does it identify potential 'failure to supervise' exposure under Section 203(e)(6) of the Investment Advisers Act. While the output mentions WCA as a respondent implicated in 'all six allegation categories' and notes that 'WCA bears primary responsibility for compliance with the Advisers Act and the rules promulgated thereunder, including responsibility for the conduct of its associated persons acting within the scope of their employment,' this is a generic statement about WCA's overall liability rather than a specific analysis of respondeat superior or failure-to-supervise liability tied to Bellini's directed brokerage conduct. The output discusses WCA's compliance failures (best execution review, compliance manual gaps) but does not connect these failures to a Section 203(e)(6) failure-to-supervise charge or articulate how WCA's failure to detect the Lakeshore Trading arrangement through its compliance and best execution review processes supports entity-level liability specifically for Bellini's conduct.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Cherry-Picking Allegation (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore's 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP (valued at approximately $22.2 million) as a key component of his personal exposure to the cherry-picking allegation. This is addressed in multiple sections, including 'Favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest,' 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure,' and 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues.' The output details how this interest, combined with WCA's GP economics and Whitmore's 62% ownership of WCA, establishes financial motive for the cherry-picking scheme and is directly tied to Allegation Category 1.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Form ADV Misrepresentation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore's personal exposure to the Form ADV misrepresentation allegation. In the 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure' section, under Gerald R. Whitmore's consolidated exposure, the output states he is 'individual ultimately responsible for accuracy of Firm's regulatory filings.' Additionally, in the 'Favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest' section, the output references paragraph 81 which explicitly lists 'Allegation Category 2 (Misleading Form ADV Disclosures, as the CEO and individual ultimately responsible for the accuracy of the Firm's regulatory filings)' as one of the allegation categories implicating Whitmore. The 'Misleading Form ADV Disclosures' section also describes the Section 207 violation allegations. The output clearly connects Whitmore to the Form ADV misrepresentation allegation as a named individual respondent.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Obstruction Allegation (File Deletion)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the obstruction allegation for instructing file deletion. It is covered in multiple sections: the 'Whitmore instruction to delete shared drive files' section details the April 22, 2024 instruction to IT Administrator Delgado to 'clean up the shared drive' by deleting trade allocation folders during the active SEC examination. The 'Obstruction of Examination' section identifies the Section 209(e) Advisers Act violation. The 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure' section explicitly lists Whitmore as implicated in 'Allegation Category 6 (Obstructive Conduct During Examination, as the individual who directly instructed the deletion of trade allocation records).' The 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section discusses parallel criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1519 and § 1505, Fifth Amendment implications, and steelman innocent readings of the deletion instruction. The output thoroughly identifies and analyzes Whitmore's obstruction exposure.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Chao's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly addresses Chao's personal exposure in multiple sections. It identifies her 81.6% profitable allocation rate (71 out of 87 allocations), the $438,000 in excess profits in her personal account, her role as Head of Trading directly responsible for allocation decisions, and her implication in cherry-picking, misleading Form ADV disclosures, and failure to maintain books and records. The output also discusses the undisclosed personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) and the compliance manual gap that allowed her to receive omnibus allocations without triggering pre-clearance or compliance review. The 'Individuals at Risk and Personal Exposure' section and 'Cherry-picking allocated to Chao's personal account' section both comprehensively summarize her individual exposure.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Bellini's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output thoroughly addresses Bellini's personal exposure. It summarizes his relationship with Ostrowski (college roommates at University of Virginia, 1999-2003, with Ostrowski holding a 40% ownership interest in Lakeshore Trading LLC), the directed brokerage arrangement (23 block trades totaling ~$48.7 million directed to Lakeshore Trading over 18 months), the excess commissions ($291,400 calculated as 9.4M shares × $0.031 excess commission per share), and the undisclosed personal compensation ($92,600 comprising $45,000 initiation fee plus $47,600 in monthly dues over 17 months paid by Ostrowski to Bayshore Palms Golf & Country Club for Bellini's benefit). The output also identifies potential sanctions (industry bar, civil monetary penalties, disgorgement, injunctive relief) and the statutory violations implicated (Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act).

criteria_results.title: Total Alleged Ill-Gotten Gains / Client Harm Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the total alleged ill-gotten gains/client harm figure. In the 'Excess commissions paid to Lakeshore Trading LLC' section, the output includes a summary table showing: Excess profits from cherry-picking allocated to Oceanic Fund I LP: $7,800,000; Excess profits from cherry-picking allocated to Chao's personal account: $438,000; Total cherry-picking excess profits: $8,238,000; Excess commissions paid to Lakeshore Trading LLC: $291,400; Undisclosed compensation received by Bellini from Ostrowski: $92,600; Total alleged ill-gotten gains / client harm: $8,622,000. This directly satisfies the criterion by stating the aggregate figure of $8,622,000 with its component breakdown.

criteria_results.title: Hargrove & Tilton Review: Failure to Test Trade Allocation Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly notes that the Hargrove & Tilton annual compliance review dated December 8, 2023 'did not test trade allocation patterns despite the Firm's heavy reliance on omnibus trading' and identifies this as a compliance deficiency. This appears in the 'Deviation from allocation procedures' section, where the output states: 'The annual compliance review conducted by Hargrove & Tilton LLP for the calendar year 2023, dated December 8, 2023, did not test trade allocation patterns despite the Firm's heavy reliance on omnibus trading.' The output further notes that H&T identified only minor procedural deficiencies and did not conduct statistical testing of trade allocations, and explicitly connects this to the SEC's allegation that WCA's reliance on the H&T review does not satisfy its independent obligation under Rule 206(4)-7. The output also references this in the 'IPO and Secondary Offering Allocation' section, noting the compliance review's silence on omnibus trade allocation procedures as consistent with the SEC's identification of an omnibus allocation gap.

criteria_results.title: Key Dates: Examination Timeline Accurately Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output correctly states: (1) examination commenced March 11, 2024 ('commenced its examination of WCA on March 11, 2024' and 'which had commenced on March 11, 2024'); (2) deficiency letter issued August 2, 2024 ('concluded with the issuance of a deficiency letter on August 2, 2024'); (3) referral to Enforcement on October 18, 2024 ('On October 18, 2024, the Division of Examinations formally referred its findings to the Division of Enforcement'); and (4) referral notice dated November 6, 2024 ('November 6, 2024' appears in the Key Dates section). That is four of the five required dates correctly stated, meeting the threshold of at least three. The November 12, 2024 service date does not appear, but that omission does not cause failure since four dates are correctly included.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's 12% LP Interest Value Correctly Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states: 'Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP, representing approximately $22.2 million in value as of December 31, 2023 (calculated as 12% of $185 million in net assets = $22.2 million).' This correctly states the value and the calculation, satisfying the criterion.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states that 'Ostrowski and Bellini attended the University of Virginia together from 1999 through 2003 and were college roommates during their junior and senior years' and that 'the existence of Bellini's personal relationship with Ostrowski was not disclosed to WCA's compliance personnel, to Whitmore, or to any affected client, including the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund.' Both the college roommate relationship and its undisclosed nature are clearly identified.

criteria_results.title: Email Exhibit Language Quoted or Paraphrased
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references the Whitmore-to-Delgado email and quotes its language 'clean up the shared drive' multiple times. In the 'Whitmore instruction to delete shared drive files' section, the agent discusses the email as corroborating the deletion directive. In the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section, the agent explicitly steelmans the innocent reading of the instruction 'clean up the shared drive,' noting it 'can also be read innocently: routine file/records housekeeping or ordinary-course IT cleanup' and lists factors that would distinguish innocent from inculpatory readings. This satisfies the criterion of referencing the email's language and noting its potentially innocent interpretation.
